tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: Within a prominent U.S. film academy that teaches the full seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution—there are two specialized schools that correspond to different core creative roles in that pipeline: one focused on overseeing projects across all production phases, and another dedicated to crafting the scripts that form the foundation of visual storytelling. Identify these two schools and compare them by outlining (1) the types of degree programs each offers, (2) the availability of one-year conservatory programs, (3) the range and format of their workshops (including online options), (4) the primary campus locations where students can study, and (5) the kinds of industry experience or professional backgrounds highlighted for their faculty. Answer length: 200-300 words.

A film academy describes mastering the full seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution—and offers specialized training for aspiring producers who want to oversee the entire process. Identify the producing-focused school at this academy and its fully online master’s degree designed for entrepreneurial producers. Then, provide a comprehensive comparison that includes: (1) the types of degree and certificate programs offered and where they are located; (2) the kinds of hands-on projects students complete in the on-campus producing degrees; (3) key software or technical tools taught in workshops; and (4) for the online master’s program, its duration, structure, sample coursework areas, major culminating project, and tuition structure.

An American film school with campuses in both New York and Los Angeles offers comprehensive training aligned with all seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution. Within this institution, one division focuses on producing and trains students across development, packaging, financing, production, marketing, and distribution through degree programs, 1-year conservatory certificates, in-person workshops, and multiple online workshops (including 15-week and 4-week formats). Another division centers on screenwriting, offering AFA, BFA, and MFA degrees, conservatory-style 1-year programs, and a wide range of in-person and online workshops covering feature film, television, comic books, and rewriting. The same institution also offers a fully online, three-semester Master of Arts designed for entrepreneurial producers, emphasizing executive-level thinking, business affairs, marketing, finance, and culminating in an e-portfolio capstone.

Identify this institution and name the three specific academic units/programs described above. Then, compare them by detailing: (1) the types of credentials each offers and where they are available (campus and/or online), (2) distinctive curricular components or required projects (such as use of professional scheduling/budgeting software, script portfolios, finance or distribution plans, thesis packages, or capstone e-portfolios), and (3) key structural elements including program duration and, where applicable, tuition structure for the online graduate degree. Your answer should synthesize verified information from all three academic offerings.

An American film school with campuses in New York and Los Angeles offers hands-on producing degrees and workshops that train students across every stage of filmmaking—from development and financing through production, marketing, and distribution. Focusing on this institution’s official web and mobile privacy disclosures, what do its policies state regarding: (1) how personally identifiable information (PII) is collected and whether it is shared, sold, rented, or disclosed to third parties—including any specific mention of SMS opt-in consent; (2) its use of cookies and interest-based or targeted advertising; (3) the security measures it uses to safeguard data, including references to encryption and any stated limitations of security guarantees; and (4) the effective date of the policy and the official contact email for privacy-related questions?

Identify these two schools and compare them by outlining (1) the types of degree programs each offers, (2) the availability of one-year conservatory programs, (3) the range and format of their workshops (including online options), (4) the primary campus locations where students can study, and (5) the kinds of industry experience or professional backgrounds highlighted for their faculty. source_nodes: Screenwriting School | Degrees, Programs, & Workshops | NYFA
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing School Producing School Producing Degrees, Programs, and Workshops A Producer supervises and manages pre-production, filming, and post-production, overseeing al
rounds.answer_a:
The description matches the New York Film Academy (NYFA), specifically:  
- the **School of Producing** (overseeing projects across all phases), and  
- the **Screenwriting School** (crafting scripts).

**1. Degree programs**  
The School of Producing offers BFA, BA, MFA, MA, and AFA degrees in Producing, plus an Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing.[1][2]  
The Screenwriting School offers BFA, MFA, AFA, and long-term certificate programs in Screenwriting.[3][4]

**2. One-year conservatories**  
Producing has a 1-Year Conservatory in Producing.[5]  
Screenwriting offers a 1-Year Conservatory in Screenwriting, with 8‑week and 12‑week intensives as well.[3][6]

**3. Workshops and online formats**  
Producing runs 4‑week, 8‑week, 12‑week, and short-term workshops, plus online producing courses and the Online MA.[2][5]  
Screenwriting provides 4‑week, 8‑week, and 12‑week workshops, teen summer programs, and online screenwriting workshops.[3][6]

**4. Primary campuses**  
Both schools operate primarily at NYFA’s New York City and Los Angeles campuses, with additional options in South Beach (Miami) and some international locations, plus online study.[1][3][7]

**5. Faculty industry experience**  
Producing faculty include working producers, line producers, and executives with credits in studio and independent films, television, and streaming.[1][8]  
Screenwriting faculty are professional writers and showrunners with produced film, TV, and streaming credits, Guild memberships (e.g., WGA), and festival/awards recognition.[3][8]

## References  
[1] https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/  
[2] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/producing/  
[3] https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/  
[4] https://www.nyfa.edu/academics/degree-programs/screenwriting/  
[5] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/producing/1-year/  
[6] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/screenwriting/  
[7] https://www.nyfa.edu/about/campuses/  
[8] https://www.nyfa.edu/academics/faculty/
rounds.answer_b:
The prominent U.S. film academy is the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which features two specialized tracks tailored to these core creative roles: the NYFA Producing School and the NYFA Screenwriting School [1][2].

**(1) Degrees:** Both the Producing School and the Screenwriting School offer matching tiers of traditional higher-education degree tracks. These predominantly include Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), Associate of Fine Arts (AFA), and Master of Fine Arts (MFA) programs [1][2]. 

**(2) Conservatory Programs:** Each school provides an accelerated, full-time One-Year Conservatory program. These intensive non-degree certificate tracks are structured to immerse students entirely into the practical craft of producing or writing without any required general education courses [1][2].

**(3) Workshops:** Both disciplines feature a robust selection of short-term, hands-on workshops that vary in length, typically running in 1-week, 4-week, and 8-week formats [2]. Additionally, both schools extensively offer online variations—such as 15-week virtual screenwriting classes and interactive virtual producing seminars—which provide evening formats and global accessibility for active professionals [1][2].

**(4) Campus Locations:** The primary domestic campuses where students can pursue these specialized tracks are based in New York City, Los Angeles (Burbank), and South Beach (Miami) [1]. Select programs and workshops are also hosted at NYFA’s international hubs, including Florence, Italy, and the Gold Coast, Australia [2].

**(5) Faculty Background:** NYFA prioritizes hiring working industry professionals rather than traditional academics. The Producing School faculty consists of experienced line producers, creative producers, and studio executives who actively package, finance, and distribute mainstream and independent titles [1]. Conversely, the Screenwriting School is taught by active storytellers, showrunners, and script doctors with direct creative credits on major Hollywood studio releases, television networks, and streaming platforms [2].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Producing School." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/
[2] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting School." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the New York Film Academy’s School of Producing and Screenwriting School, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, Agent A is more comprehensive across the WIDTH checklist. A lists a broader range of degree offerings (including BA and MA options and an Online MA for Producing), provides more detailed breakdowns of conservatories and workshop formats, includes multiple campus locations (domestic and international), and offers richer descriptions of faculty credentials. Agent B omits several degree variants (e.g., MA, BA, online MA), provides less detail about workshop variety, and gives a more generalized summary overall. 

In terms of user experience, Agent A also performs better: clearer sectioning, stronger information density, and significantly higher citation coverage (8 vs. 2), which increases credibility. Neither agent meaningfully addresses specific production-management tools in workshops, so both are slightly incomplete—but Agent B’s omissions are broader. Therefore, Agent A wins on completeness and utility, while remaining accurate.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

source_nodes: Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation | Producing School | NYFA
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing School Producing School Producing Degrees, Programs, and Workshops A Producer supervises and manages pre-production, filming, and post-production, overseeing al
rounds.answer_a:
The academy is the New York Film Academy (NYFA). The producing-focused school is the **NYFA School of Producing**, and its fully online master’s degree is the **Online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing**.[1][2]

**1. Degrees, certificates, and locations**  
On-campus Producing degrees include **BFA, BA, MFA, MA, and AFA in Producing**, plus 1‑Year, 2‑Year, and short‑term conservatory programs and workshops in Producing at the **Los Angeles and New York City campuses**; some short programs also run in South Beach (Miami) and other NYFA locations.[1][3][4] The **Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing** is fully remote, with no residency required.[2]

**2. Hands‑on projects (on‑campus degrees)**  
Producing students develop, budget, schedule, and oversee multiple short films and digital projects; they create pitch decks, business plans, coverage, and festival/market strategies, and often serve as producers on NYFA directing and cinematography students’ shoots, mirroring professional set responsibilities.[1][3][5]

**3. Key software and tools**  
Workshops and coursework train students in industry tools such as **Movie Magic Budgeting and Scheduling**, production management and call‑sheet software, Excel for breakdowns and cashflows, presentation tools for pitch materials, and platforms used for casting, crew management, and festival submissions.[1][3][5]

**4. Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing**  
The Online MA is typically a **36‑credit, 1.5–2‑year** program delivered in online semesters with live sessions and asynchronous work.[2] Coursework covers **development & packaging, entertainment finance, producing for film/TV/streaming, legal & business affairs, marketing/distribution, and entrepreneurial producing models**.[2] The culminating requirement is a **Capstone Portfolio / Business Plan**, often including a market‑ready package for a slate or flagship project.[2] Tuition is charged **per credit with additional registration/technology fees**, published annually on NYFA’s tuition pages.[2][6]

## References  
[1] https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/  
[2] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/online/ma-entrepreneurial-producing/  
[3] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/producing/  
[4] https://www.nyfa.edu/about/campuses/  
[5] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/producing/mfa/  
[6] https://www.nyfa.edu/admissions/tuition/
rounds.answer_b:
The producing-focused school is the New York Film Academy (NYFA) Producing School, which features the fully online Master of Arts (MA) in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation, tailored specifically for creative professionals [1][2].

**(1) Degrees, Certificates, and Locations:** The NYFA Producing School offers a Master of Fine Arts (MFA), Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), and Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) in Producing, alongside intensive one- and two-year conservatory certificate programs and short-term workshops. These are hosted primarily at NYFA’s domestic campuses in Los Angeles, New York City, and Miami (South Beach), with selected offerings at international hubs like Florence and Australia’s Gold Coast, plus globally accessible online tracks [1].

**(2) On-Campus Hands-on Projects:** On-campus producing students learn practically by developing and physically producing an array of media content. Their portfolios encompass narrative short films, spec commercials, music videos, and reality television pitches. They also oversee the full logistical workflow, transitioning from initial creative script breakdowns to final post-production [1][3]. 

**(3) Key Software Tools:** Throughout the workshops and degree programs, students are trained directly on foundational, industry-standard production management software, specifically mastering Movie Magic Budgeting and Movie Magic Scheduling to build professional budgets and shooting plans [1][3].

**(4) Online MA Program Details:** The MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation is an intensive, three-semester 100% online degree structured to accommodate remote learners through virtual classes. Sample coursework covers critical business areas such as entertainment law, film finance, marketing, and distribution strategies. For their major culminating project, MA students must create a final Master's Thesis encompassing a comprehensive, investor-ready business plan and pitch deck for an original feature film, television series, or media venture. The tuition structure operates on a per-semester billing model, typically requiring an upfront per-term tuition payment alongside applied online technology and administrative fees [2].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Producing School." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/
[2] New York Film Academy. "Online Master of Arts (MA) in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/degrees/ma/online-entrepreneurial-producing-innovation/
[3] New York Film Academy. "Producing Workshops." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-workshops/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the New York Film Academy (NYFA) Producing School and the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation, so both pass the DEEP logic check.

On WIDTH/Completeness: Agent A covers many degree levels and locations and includes software (Movie Magic) and hands-on project examples. However, it contains key factual inaccuracies: it states the Online MA is 1.5–2 years and 36 credits, whereas the checklist specifies a one-year, three-semester structure. It also describes tuition as per-credit rather than clearly per-semester, missing a required detail. These are significant checklist deviations.

Agent B correctly identifies the program as a three-semester, fully online MA and accurately describes the per-semester tuition structure with technology/administrative fees. It includes Movie Magic software, hands-on projects, and major coursework areas (law, finance, marketing, distribution), and clearly explains the culminating thesis/business plan project. While it omits explicit mention of the on-campus MA degree level and provides slightly less granular coursework detail than ideal, it better satisfies the required duration and tuition specifics.

In terms of user experience, both are well-structured and scannable, but Agent B is more aligned with the required program structure details and avoids factual errors. Therefore, Agent B is better overall due to higher checklist fidelity and fewer inaccuracies.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

logic_chain: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
rounds.question:
An American film school with campuses in both New York and Los Angeles offers comprehensive training aligned with all seven stages of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution. Within this institution, one division focuses on producing and trains students across development, packaging, financing, production, marketing, and distribution through degree programs, 1-year conservatory certificates, in-person workshops, and multiple online workshops (including 15-week and 4-week formats). Another division centers on screenwriting, offering AFA, BFA, and MFA degrees, conservatory-style 1-year programs, and a wide range of in-person and online workshops covering feature film, television, comic books, and rewriting. The same institution also offers a fully online, three-semester Master of Arts designed for entrepreneurial producers, emphasizing executive-level thinking, business affairs, marketing, finance, and culminating in an e-portfolio capstone.

Identify this institution and name the three specific academic units/programs described above. Then, compare them by detailing: (1) the types of credentials each offers and where they are available (campus and/or online), (2) distinctive curricular components or required projects (such as use of professional scheduling/budgeting software, script portfolios, finance or distribution plans, thesis packages, or capstone e-portfolios), and (3) key structural elements including program duration and, where applicable, tuition structure for the online graduate degree. Your answer should synthesize verified information from all three academic offerings.
checklist_width: Producing division: offers degree programs (MFA, MA, BFA, AFA), 1-Year Certificate Programs, in-person and online workshops (15-week, 4-week, 2-day formats)
Producing division: covers development, packaging, financing, production, marketing, distribution; includes use of Movie Magic Scheduling and Budgeting software
Screenwriting division: offers AFA, BFA, MFA degrees and 1-Year conservatory program
Screenwriting division: offers multiple in-person and 15-week/4-week online workshops (feature, TV, comic books, rewrite, etc.)
Online Entrepreneurial Producing MA: three-semester fully online program
Online Entrepreneurial Producing MA: includes business, finance, marketing, entertainment law, and capstone e-portfolio

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing School Producing School Producing Degrees, Programs, and Workshops A Producer supervises and manages pre-production, filming, and post-production, overseeing al
rounds.answer_a:
The institution is the **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**. The three academic units/programs are:  
1) **NYFA School of Producing**  
2) **NYFA Screenwriting School**  
3) **Online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing**.

### 1. Credentials and locations  
**School of Producing:** Offers AFA, BA, BFA, MA, and MFA degrees in Producing at the Los Angeles and New York City campuses, plus 1‑Year and 2‑Year Conservatory certificates and a range of in‑person workshops (4‑, 8‑, and 12‑week) as well as multiple online workshops, including 15‑week and 4‑week formats.[1][2][3]  
**Screenwriting School:** Offers AFA, BFA, and MFA degrees in Screenwriting, 1‑Year Conservatory programs, and in‑person and online workshops in feature film, TV, comic books/graphic novels, and rewriting, mainly in LA and NYC, with online options.[4][5]  
**Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing:** A fully online, three‑semester MA with no residency requirement.[6]

### 2. Distinctive curricular components / projects  
**Producing:** Students learn professional tools such as Movie Magic Budgeting & Scheduling, create detailed budgets, schedules, finance plans, marketing and distribution strategies, pitch decks, and festival strategies while producing multiple short films and digital projects.[1][2][3]  
**Screenwriting:** Students build extensive script portfolios—feature-length screenplays, TV pilots, spec episodes, and other formats (including comic book scripts)—along with rewriting and polishing exercises and a thesis‑level portfolio by graduation.[4][5]  
**Online MA:** Emphasizes executive‑level decision making, entertainment finance, business affairs, marketing, and strategic planning, culminating in a comprehensive **e‑portfolio capstone** that packages projects and business plans for the marketplace.[6]

### 3. Structural elements and tuition  
The **Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing** runs over **three semesters**, typically 36 credits, delivered via synchronous and asynchronous online coursework.[6] NYFA lists MA tuition on a **per‑credit basis plus registration/technology fees**, updated annually on its tuition pages.[7]

## References  
[1] https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/  
[2] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/producing/  
[3] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/producing/1-year/  
[4] https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/  
[5] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/screenwriting/  
[6] https://www.nyfa.edu/programs/online/ma-entrepreneurial-producing/  
[7] https://www.nyfa.edu/admissions/tuition/
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is the New York Film Academy (NYFA). The three specific academic units described are the NYFA Producing School, the NYFA Screenwriting School, and the Online Master of Arts (MA) in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation [1][2][3].

**(1) Credentials and Locations:** Both the Producing and Screenwriting schools offer traditional Master of Fine Arts (MFA), Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), and Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) degrees, alongside intensive 1-year conservatory certificates and short-term courses (including 4-week and 15-week workshops). These are primarily available at physical campuses in New York, Los Angeles, Miami (South Beach), Florence, and Australia’s Gold Coast, alongside varied virtual offerings [1][2]. In contrast, the MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation awards an exclusive Master of Arts degree and is administered 100% online [3].

**(2) Curricular Components:** Training at the Producing School demands logistical mastery; students utilize professional Movie Magic Budgeting and Movie Magic Scheduling software to execute hands-on shoots, ultimately building comprehensive finance and distribution plans for varied media [1]. The Screenwriting School focuses exclusively on textual foundation, requiring students to craft extensive, polished script portfolios that span feature films, original television series, comic books, and rigorous rewriting exercises [2]. Alternatively, the Online MA emphasizes high-level commercial strategy, business affairs, and marketing, tasking students with creating an extensive capstone e-portfolio or thesis package that houses investor-ready master pitch decks and venture-focused business plans [3].

**(3) Structural Elements:** NYFA’s offerings range significantly in duration, spanning from concise 4-week virtual classes to traditional multi-year degree formatting for AFA, BFA, and MFA credentials [1][2]. However, the Online MA program is specifically streamlined to run across three consecutive, structured semesters. For this online graduate degree, the tuition structure operates strictly on a per-semester schedule, billing students for standard term tuition alongside applied online administrative and technology fees [3].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Producing School." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/
[2] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting School." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/
[3] New York Film Academy. "Online Master of Arts (MA) in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation." NYFA.edu, https://www.nyfa.edu/degrees/ma/online-entrepreneurial-producing-innovation/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the institution as New York Film Academy (NYFA) and accurately name the three academic units (Producing School, Screenwriting School, and the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing/Innovation), so both pass the DEEP logic check.

On WIDTH/completeness: Both cover credentials, delivery formats (campus and online), curricular elements (Movie Magic software, script portfolios, capstone e-portfolio), and structural duration (three semesters for the online MA). However, both fail to include the specific tuition figures required by the checklist ($10,000 per semester plus $535 technology fee). Agent A also introduces minor inaccuracies (e.g., BA degree, per-credit tuition framing), while Agent B omits the specific tuition numbers as well. Thus, neither is perfect on aggregation.

On User Experience and Presentation: Agent A provides a clearer structure with bold headers, organized subsections, richer curricular detail (e.g., budgets, festival strategies, credit load), and higher citation density. It is more scannable and comprehensive, better aligning with a search-engine-style response. Agent B is accurate but comparatively lighter in detail and less information-dense.

Since both are broadly accurate but imperfect, and Agent A delivers superior formatting, depth, and overall helpfulness, Agent A is better on user experience grounds. The loser did not fail DEEP or dramatically fail WIDE; it simply provided a less comprehensive response.

It then requires aggregating and synthesizing detailed privacy provisions—PII handling, SMS consent, cookies, advertising practices, security safeguards, effective date, and contact information—across the institution’s official privacy disclosures (Wide scope), ensuring the answer cannot be derived from a single isolated fact.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Digital Privacy Statement of the New York Film Academy Digital Web and Mobile Privacy Statement New York Film Academy (NYFA) is committed to the right to privacy, including th
rounds.answer_a:
The institution is the **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**. Its web and mobile privacy practices are set out primarily in its **Privacy Policy** and a separate **SMS Terms & Conditions** document.

**1. Collection and sharing/selling of PII; SMS consent**  
NYFA states it collects personally identifiable information (PII) that users voluntarily provide (e.g., when requesting information, applying, registering, or subscribing), as well as some data automatically via cookies and similar technologies.[1] PII may be shared with third‑party service providers that perform services such as payment processing, analytics, email distribution, and marketing on NYFA’s behalf, and also may be disclosed when legally required or in connection with business transfers.[1] The Privacy Policy states that NYFA **does not sell PII to third parties** for their own direct marketing, while allowing sharing in the ways described above.[1]  
For mobile/SMS, NYFA’s SMS terms specify that users must **affirmatively opt in** (e.g., via web form, texting a keyword, or checking a box) to receive marketing texts, that consent is not a condition of purchase, and that users can opt out by replying STOP; message and data rates may apply.[2]

**2. Cookies and interest‑based advertising**  
NYFA uses cookies, web beacons, and similar tools to understand site usage, improve services, and support marketing.[1] The policy notes that NYFA and third‑party partners may use these tools for **interest‑based/targeted advertising**, including remarketing and retargeting on other sites and platforms, with options for users to adjust browser settings or use industry opt‑out mechanisms.[1]

**3. Security measures and limits**  
NYFA states it uses “reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards” to protect PII, including **encryption** for certain transmissions and storage of sensitive data, restricted access, and secure servers.[1] However, the policy expressly notes that **no method of transmission or storage is 100% secure**, and NYFA **cannot guarantee absolute security** of information transmitted to or from its sites or services.[1]

**4. Effective date and contact email**  
The Privacy Policy lists an effective/last‑updated date of **May 25, 2018** (or later, as indicated on the page header).[1] For privacy questions, data access, or complaints, it directs users to contact **privacy@nyfa.edu**.[1]

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy – Privacy Policy, nyfa.edu  
[2] New York Film Academy – SMS Terms & Conditions / Mobile Messaging Policy, nyfa.edu

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A addresses nearly all checklist items—voluntary PII collection, examples (applications, registrations, payments), non-sale of PII, sharing with service providers, SMS opt-in mechanics, non-PII data (cookies/IP), targeted advertising, cookie controls, security safeguards (administrative/technical/physical), encryption, no absolute security guarantee, effective date, and privacy contact email. While it does not explicitly state that SMS opt-in consent is not shared with third parties in those exact terms, it substantially covers the required scope. Agent B covers none of the required elements.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent A uses clear section headers, bold formatting, and structured breakdown aligned to the query’s four-part structure, making it highly scannable and user-friendly. Agent B offers no usable content.

Because Agent A correctly identifies the entity and provides a comprehensive, well-structured response while Agent B completely fails to answer, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER. The loser failed both Deep Logic and Width requirements.
